hazard.summary: Provider WAF block signals are hidden across the crawler admission boundary — robots.txt and sitemap success authorizes 10,000-task page fanout that exhausts retry budgets against terminal 403 blocks.
signals: Some sites put a security wall in front of their pages (Cloudflare, Imperva, DataDome, Akamai). The wall lets /robots.txt and /sitemap.xml through (those are designed to be machine-readable), so we happily build a 10,000-task job — then every page hits the wall and 403s. We retry, exhaust, and bill the time anyway.
Add response-fingerprint checks in the crawler response handler (cf-mitigated: header, _Incapsula_Resource body marker, Server: DataDome, tiny-body-with-403/202).
Terminal failures (WAF, soft-block, 4xx, parse error) stop retrying entirely.
failure_class enum: dns, tcp, tls, http_4xx, http_5xx, http_429, waf, soft_block, ttfb_timeout, body_timeout, parse_error.

failure_details JSONB carries: HTTP status, selected response headers (server, cf-ray, cf-mitigated, retry-after, content-length, content-type), detection markers triggered, body snippet (512 B, sanitised), Go error class, timing breakdown (DNS/TCP/TLS/TTFB/total).
Akamai 403s any non-browser UA including Googlebot — real Googlebot only gets through because Akamai verifies it via reverse-DNS / IP allowlist, not the UA string.
373/377-byte HTML body on a 403 (classic EdgeSuite block page)

constraints: Crawler admission must not treat robots.txt or sitemap availability as sufficient evidence that page fetches are crawlable.
Crawler admission must collect a representative page-scope provider response before enqueueing high-cardinality page tasks.
Provider block fingerprints must propagate from HTTP response handling into job admission, task failure classification, and retry policy.
Terminal WAF or soft-block signals must not consume transient retry budgets.
Job runners must short-circuit remaining tasks when an early page sample repeatedly matches terminal WAF fingerprints.
Failure reporting must distinguish domain-blocked terminal outcomes from generic task failure.
verification: Setup → Mock a domain where /robots.txt and /sitemap.xml return 200, but the homepage returns 403 with `cf-mitigated` WAF evidence. Action → Start a crawl for the domain. Assertion → The crawler marks the domain blocked before bulk page enqueue, creates no 10,000-task page fanout, and exposes a domain-blocked job status.

Setup → Mock a domain that passes admission but whose first N page responses return 403 with `Server: AkamaiGHost`, `Set-Cookie: akaalb_*`, and a classic small EdgeSuite block body. Action → Run the job runner through the first N page tasks. Assertion → The circuit breaker marks the domain blocked, short-circuits remaining tasks, and does not retry the terminal WAF failures.

Setup → Feed the response handler 403/202 responses containing `cf-mitigated: header`, `_Incapsula_Resource`, `Server: DataDome`, and Akamai signatures. Action → Classify each response. Assertion → Each response is assigned `failure_class = waf` and stores status, selected headers, detection markers, and sanitized body snippet in failure details.

Setup → Configure one task failure as `failure_class = waf` and another as `failure_class = http_429` with `retry-after`. Action → Apply retry policy. Assertion → The WAF task receives zero retries as terminal, while the 429 task uses class-specific backoff and honours `retry-after`.

Setup → Run a crawl against a domain where robots/sitemap access succeeds but all page fetches return WAF markers. Action → Inspect persisted task and job records after completion. Assertion → The job records a domain-blocked terminal outcome instead of only generic `failed` task states, and retry counts remain below the transient retry budget.
notes: Dominant mechanism: WAF block signals are hidden at admission and retry-classification boundaries. robots.txt and sitemap availability are necessary but not sufficient for page crawlability — a page-scope preflight probe and WAF fingerprint classifier are required before high-cardinality task enqueue. The 429 failure class is part of the system's proposed failure taxonomy but is secondary to the WAF admission failure. Related PRs: #366 (UA rename), #367 (WAF detection/blocking).

constraints: Crawler admission must validate page-scope reachability before high-cardinality enqueue.
Terminal block signals must be classified before retry policy is applied.
Provider block evidence must remain visible across response handling, job orchestration, and user-facing status.
anti_patterns: Using robots.txt or sitemap success as proof of page crawl viability.
Enqueueing high-cardinality page tasks before representative provider-block detection.
Retrying WAF and 4xx terminal failures with the same policy as transient DNS, TCP, 5xx, or 429 failures.
Continuing user-agent tweaking after WAF fingerprints show a non-browser or allowlist block.
Collapsing DNS, TCP, TLS, WAF, soft-block, timeout, parse, and HTTP failures into a generic failed state.
